chain_of_thought: Native crash is a memmove failure inside libbson-c-lib.so, with top app frame byte_array_to_bson_string, then bson_object_from_bytes, then JNI bridge Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes.

Tools are unavailable (404) so I cannot inspect the actual implementation, but the symbol names and crash site strongly suggest a buffer move/copy involving a byte array converted to a BSON string/object.

The memmove crash implies an out-of-bounds read/write or overlapping/invalid regions, which in typical BSON libraries arises when parsing length fields from untrusted byte arrays without full validation.

The data source here is a Java byte[] passed through the JNI bridge into bson_object_from_bytes and then byte_array_to_bson_string; under realistic use this byte[] is likely attacker-influenced (e.g., network or file data encoded as BSON), and the fuzzer is simulating exactly such untrusted inputs.

Given a generic BSON-from-bytes API, robust code must treat the input as untrusted and thoroughly validate lengths and offsets before memmove; a crash indicates missing/insufficient validation, which is a classical memory-corruption pattern (CWE-787/788).

No evidence suggests this is a harness-only issue like forcing impossible invariants (e.g., NULL env, broken JNIEnv); the stack is clean and the fault is at memmove driven by library logic, not JNI boilerplate.

Therefore, it is reasonable to classify this as a likely genuine vulnerability: attacker-controlled BSON bytes can trigger memmove with invalid bounds, causing memory corruption/DoS and potentially exploitable conditions depending on allocator/ASLR.

Without code, I cannot prove exact exploitability or whether bounds checks exist but rely on undefined behavior; still, the existence of a crash in memmove from arbitrary bytes gives moderately high confidence of a real bug with at least DoS impact.

chain_of_thought: Identify first app-level native frame below allocator/sanitizer: crash occurs in __memcpy, next frame is byte_array_to_bson_string in libbson-c-lib.so; this is the first relevant application-level function.

Given the minimal stack info, infer likely pattern: byte_array_to_bson_string probably converts a raw byte[] into an internal BSON string/buffer, very likely using memcpy with a length derived from BSON metadata or Java/JNI length values.

The function name suggests a direct transformation from a byte array to a BSON string without obvious intrinsic size limits; if length comes from attacker-controlled data (fuzzer or real input), an unchecked memcpy could overflow or read out of bounds, causing the crash in __memcpy.

Back-propagating taint conceptually: fuzz_one_input → Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes → bson_object_from_bytes → byte_array_to_bson_string → __memcpy. The fuzz harness controls the initial byte array contents and length; in a real app, this method would typically be fed from network/IPC/file data. No evidence of sanitization or bounds checking is provided on the native side.

Because the failure is specifically a memcpy crash, not a benign Java exception, it indicates native memory misuse (likely out-of-bounds read/write). memcpy crashes generally arise from invalid source or destination pointers/lengths, commonly due to insufficient length checks or trusting length fields embedded in the data.

The presence of a dedicated parser function bson_object_from_bytes strongly suggests the code reads internal length fields from the BSON payload; if those are not validated against the actual buffer size (the JNI-provided length), an attacker can craft inconsistent lengths and cause memcpy to operate past the end of the buffer (CWE-787/125).

No signs point to a pure harness misuse like passing null or obviously invalid pointers; the call stack indicates a standard JNI mapping from a Java byte[] into libbson. The same interface is likely reachable from normal application flows since BsonEncoder is a first-party class.

Even without decompiled code, the combination of: (1) memcpy-based crash, (2) parsing of attacker-controlled binary format, and (3) no evidence of bounds checks, makes it highly plausible this is a real memory safety bug rather than a harness-only artifact.

Severity-wise, an out-of-bounds memcpy in a BSON parsing library exposed to external data commonly leads to memory corruption and potential RCE; at minimum it is a high-severity native memory corruption issue. However, without full code we cannot confirm direct exploitability, so treat as high severity with less than full (but still strong) confidence.

chain_of_thought: Native stack shows crash in memmove called from byte_array_to_bson_string, then bson_object_from_bytes, then JNI bridge Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes. This is a BSON encoder/decoder library; fuzz harness fuzz_one_input is clearly feeding arbitrary bytes into the BSON parsing routine via JNI. Memmove crash strongly suggests out-of-bounds read/write or overlap issue; in practice, most memmove crashes from fuzzing BSON-like libraries are due to using attacker-controlled lengths or indexes without adequate bounds checks while walking the encoded structure.

Library map indicates all relevant functions (byte_array_to_bson_string, bson_object_from_bytes, JNI bridge) live in libbson-c-lib.so. We attempted to search for these functions in Ghidra but tool search failed (404), so we cannot show exact decompiled snippets. However, the function names are self-descriptive: bson_object_from_bytes and bson_array_from_bytes will parse user-supplied byte arrays into internal BSON objects, and byte_array_to_bson_string likely converts encoded bytes to a string representation. Memmove inside that conversion is almost certainly moving slices of the attacker-controlled buffer or an output buffer sized based on fields in the encoded stream.

The JNI bridge Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes exposes this parser directly to Java. BsonEncoder.bson_object_from_bytes (by naming convention) will be a public API taking a byte[] from Java and handing it to the JNI function without additional verification of internal structure. The fuzz harness uses fuzz_one_input as entry and then calls the JNI, mimicking realistic use where arbitrary or remote data (e.g., network messages) can reach this API. Therefore, the input is attacker-controlled and realistically reachable in production. No evidence suggests production would only ever pass trusted, well-formed data.

Memmove usually only aborts when given invalid pointers/lengths, which is not something memmove validates semantically: it assumes dst/src pointer ranges are valid in-process but will crash on out-of-bounds access. That in turn depends on library code computing an incorrect length. In parsing variable-length BSON fields, lengths come from encoded size headers inside the user-controlled buffer. A common bug pattern: read a length from the buffer, trust it blindly, and then memmove that many bytes either within the same buffer or into a stack/heap buffer with fewer bytes allocated, leading to heap buffer overflow or read beyond end of buffer. Given the fuzzing context and memmove crash, this is the most plausible mechanism.

We cannot see the exact decompilation, so we cannot name the specific variable or show the missing bounds check, but all available signals (memmove, parser from bytes, fuzz-induced crash) favour a genuine memory safety issue (CWE-787/788). Even if it were solely an out-of-bounds read, it’s still memory corruption / potential info leak; if dst is in heap/stack, it can be an overflow. libbson-c-lib is native C code, so such issues are exploitable in principle for code execution or at least denial of service. There is no sign this is due to an artificial harness artifact like passing NULL env or impossible Java types.

Therefore, this crash is best classified as a likely genuine vulnerability: an out-of-bounds memory operation in libbson-c-lib’s byte_array_to_bson_string / bson_*_from_bytes parser functions, reachable from Java through JNI with attacker-controlled data. Confidence is not maximal because we lack direct Ghidra decomp evidence, but the pattern is strong enough for ~0.7. Severity should be high due to native memory corruption in a parser of externally provided data.

Input bytes originate from fuzz_one_input via JNI method Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes, so attacker controls buffer contents and embedded length fields.
Function names (bson_array_from_bytes, bson_object_from_bytes) and lack of visible checks strongly suggest unvalidated length/offset calculations leading to out-of-bounds access or overflow.
Pattern matches typical BSON/serialization parsing bugs where claimed string/array length is trusted and passed to memmove without bounding against actual buffer size.
recommendations: Audit byte_array_to_bson_string, bson_array_from_bytes, and bson_object_from_bytes for all memmove/memcpy-style calls and ensure lengths are bounded by actual source/destination buffer sizes.
Add strict validation of all length and offset fields in BSON input before performing copies or allocations; reject inputs with inconsistent or oversized lengths.
Compile with address sanitizers and run targeted fuzzing on BSON parsing entrypoints (including the JNI method) to confirm and localize the exact out-of-bounds condition, then add regression tests.
assumptions: The memmove crash is due to invalid src/dst/len rather than an unrelated allocator or OS fault.
BSON parsing functions derive copy lengths from attacker-controlled fields in the input without adequate bounds checking.
In real app usage, the BSON byte arrays can be influenced by untrusted external data (network, files, or IPC), not only by the fuzzer.
limitations: No decompiled native code or exact fault address/exception type was available to pinpoint precise src/dst/len expressions.
Java-side argument construction and any potential pre-validation before JNI were not directly inspected.
The exact exploitability (e.g., controllable overwrite vs. read OOB) cannot be determined from the stack trace alone.

chain_of_thought: Top native frame is __memcpy, so I move one frame down to byte_array_to_bson_string as the first app-level function. I cannot decompile due to MCP 404 errors, so I reason from symbols and stack only.

The crash in __memcpy implies a memory safety issue in the caller: typically out-of-bounds read/write or passing invalid pointers/length. The function name byte_array_to_bson_string suggests copying from a Java-provided byte array into a BSON buffer/string, very likely using memcpy.

The call sequence byte_array_to_bson_string → bson_array_from_bytes → bson_object_from_bytes → JNI indicates that arbitrary attacker-controlled bytes (from fuzz_one_input and, in the app, from untrusted data sources) are converted to BSON without guaranteed prior validation.

Because __memcpy crashed, it is highly likely that the length passed to memcpy exceeded the actual destination or source buffer size, or a bad pointer was computed. That’s a classic buffer overflow / OOB access scenario, especially in parsing libraries handling variable-length encodings like BSON.

The library is part of the app (libbson-c-lib.so) and exposes general-purpose parsing APIs to Java. There is no evidence that JNI performs stringent bounds checking on the input size or contents before calling bson_object_from_bytes/bson_array_from_bytes, and typical fuzz harnesses mirror realistic use by just passing arbitrary buffers to those JNI wrappers.

Even though I cannot see the exact code, the symbol names strongly imply that memcpy is used to copy user-provided bytes into internal BSON structures based on length fields encoded in the data. If those lengths are not fully validated against the overall buffer size, malformed input will cause memcpy to go out of bounds. That matches this crash pattern and is a known class of vulnerabilities in binary parsers.

Since this is a parser reachable from Java and passed arbitrary byte[] buffers, a remote attacker could likely trigger the same bug with crafted network or file input, causing memory corruption or at least a reliable crash (DoS). That is a genuine vulnerability rather than a harness-only artifact.

Given lack of direct code view, I can’t be absolutely certain if this is read or write OOB, but any memcpy misuse from untrusted data is at least CWE-787 / CWE-120 territory. I will classify as vulnerable with medium confidence and high severity because of likely memory corruption in core parsing code.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy called by byte_array_to_bson_string while parsing attacker-controlled bytes into BSON structures
Call chain from JNI (Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes) processes arbitrary byte[] without visible bounds enforcement, typical pattern for parser overflows
Memcopy-based parsing of binary formats using embedded length fields is a known source of OOB read/write vulnerabilities and matches the observed stack trace
recommendations: Manually review and instrument byte_array_to_bson_string/bson_array_from_bytes/bson_object_from_bytes to ensure all memcpy lengths are validated against source and destination buffer sizes derived from the overall input length
Add defensive bounds checks on BSON length fields before performing any copies, and reject or truncate malformed inputs
Reproduce under ASan/valgrind with minimized inputs from the fuzzer to precisely locate the failing memcpy and confirm whether it is an OOB read or write
assumptions: JNI bridge passes byte[] directly to native BSON parsing functions without strong size or structural validation
The fuzzer input format is representative of realistic untrusted inputs the app may process (network or file data)
The crash in __memcpy is due to invalid length or pointer arguments from app code rather than an allocator or harness bug
limitations: Could not decompile libbson-c-lib.so functions due to MCP search/decompile errors, so evidence is based on symbols and crash pattern only
Exact nature of the memory error (read vs write, size, exploitability) is not confirmed without code or sanitizer output
Java-side call graph and validation logic for BsonEncoder are not inspected, so some validation may exist but is not visible here
